subject: he
description: Traditional masculine pronouns. Used by many men and some non-binary individuals.
example: He went to the store. I saw him there. His bag was heavy. That bag is his. He carried it himself.

subject: she
description: Traditional feminine pronouns. Used by many women and some non-binary individuals.
example: She went to the store. I saw her there. Her bag was heavy. That bag is hers. She carried it herself.

subject: they
description: Gender-neutral pronouns with a long history in the English language. Widely used by non-binary, genderqueer, and agender individuals. Singular they is grammatically accepted.
example: They went to the store. I saw them there. Their bag was heavy. That bag is theirs. They carried it themselves.

subject: ze
description: A gender-neutral neopronoun set. Ze/zir is one of the most widely recognized neopronoun sets. Sometimes written ze/hir with variant hir forms.
example: Ze went to the store. I saw zir there. Zir bag was heavy. That bag is zirs. Ze carried it zirself.

subject: ze
description: A gender-neutral neopronoun variant. Ze/hir combines 'ze' as subject with 'hir' derived from 'her', forming a distinct neutral form.
example: Ze went to the store. I saw hir there. Hir bag was heavy. That bag is hirs. Ze carried it hirself.

subject: xe
description: A neopronoun set popular in online and trans communities. Xe/xem is designed to be phonetically unambiguous and easy to learn.
example: Xe went to the store. I saw xem there. Xyr bag was heavy. That bag is xyrs. Xe carried it xemself.

subject: ey
description: Spivak pronouns, developed by Michael Spivak in 1983 as a gender-neutral alternative. Created by dropping the 'th' from 'they/them'.
example: Ey went to the store. I saw em there. Eir bag was heavy. That bag is eirs. Ey carried it emself.

subject: fae
description: Fae/faer neopronouns are inspired by faeries and fantasy, often used by those who feel a connection to fae mythology or aesthetics. Part of the broader neopronoun ecosystem.
example: Fae went to the store. I saw faer there. Faer bag was heavy. That bag is faers. Fae carried it faerself.

subject: it
description: It/its pronouns are used by some non-binary and agender individuals who choose them for themselves. Using it/its for someone who has not requested these pronouns can be dehumanizing — always follow a person's stated preferences.
example: It went to the store. I saw it there. Its bag was heavy. That bag is its. It carried it itself.

subject: per
description: Per/per neopronouns were popularized in Marge Piercy's 1976 novel 'Woman on the Edge of Time'. A minimalist neopronoun derived from 'person'.
example: Per went to the store. I saw per there. Per bag was heavy. That bag is pers. Per carried it perself.

subject: ve
description: Ve/ver neopronouns are an alternative gender-neutral set. Ve/ver is often chosen by those who want a pronoun distinct from they/them but still recognizable.
example: Ve went to the store. I saw ver there. Vis bag was heavy. That bag is vis. Ve carried it verself.

subject: any
description: Some people are comfortable with any pronouns — they do not have a preference and allow others to use whatever feels natural. This is sometimes listed as 'any/all' pronouns.
example: Ask the person directly — any pronoun works for them!
